messages.content: For this sentence:Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;
Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans board the ships.
For this sentence:
Iam nox est, ignis deficiunt.

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans board the ships.

- Aeneas: Proper noun (3rd declension) in the nominative case.
- et: Conjunction.
- Troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd declension) in the nominative case agreeing in gender and number with "Troiani" and used here as a substantive, meaning "the Trojans."
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd declension) in the accusative case and here used as the direct object of the verb, meaning "ships."
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conjugation) in the present tense and third person plural, meaning "they board."

This time include the original sentence in your answer. Also include the meaning of the word after each word. In your answer, show me only the important information about this word, and write declension or conjugation and necessary information all in abbreviations.

Original Sentence: Iam nox est, ignis deficiunt.

- Iam: Adverb meaning "already."
- nox: Noun (3rd declension) in the nominative case meaning "night."
- est: Verb (sum, esse) in the present tense and 3rd person singular. It means "is."
- ignis: Noun (3rd declension) in the nominative case meaning "fire."
- deficiunt: Verb (3rd conjugation) in the present tense and 3rd person plural. It means "they are going out" (in reference to the fire).

Original Sentence: Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans board the ships.

- Aeneas: Proper noun (3rd declension) in the nominative case meaning "Aeneas."
- et: Conjunction meaning "and."
- Troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd declension) in the nominative case meaning "Trojans." It agrees in gender and number with "Troiani" and is used as a substantive.
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd declension) in the accusative case meaning "ships." It's here used as the direct object of the verb.
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conjugation) in the present tense and 3rd person plural meaning "they are boarding."

Sentence: Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

- Aeneas: Proper noun (3rd dec. - n.) in the nom. case
- et: Coordinating conj.
- Troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd dec. - m./n. nom. pl.) used as a substantive in nom. pl.
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd dec. - f. acc. pl.) used as the direct obj. of the verb.
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conj. - pres. ind. act. 3rd p. pl.).

Good job! Let's do it better.

Your sentence is:
Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

This time, translate the sentence before you explain it word by word. Remove all the unimportant words when explaining the words, keep only what is necessary. For example, "used as nom. pl." should be written as "nom. pl.", thus keeping only necessary infromation.

Sentence: Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- Aeneas: Proper noun (3rd declension - n.) in nomine case.
- et: Conjunction.
- Troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd declension - m./n.) in n. pl.
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd declension - f.) in a. pl.
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conjugation - pres. ind. act. - 3rd pers. pl.)

Good job! Let’s do it better.

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- Aeneas: Prop. n. (3rd dec. - nom.)
- et: Conj.
- Troiani: Adj. (1st/2nd dec. - m./n. - nom. pl.)
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd dec. - f. - acc. pl.)
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conj. - pres. ind. act. - 3rd pers. pl.)

Your sentence is:

This time use small letter case for all parts of speech. Also, explain why the word is used in this form when you feel it is necessary.

Sentence: aeneas et troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- aeneas: Proper noun (3rd declension - nominative case) - used as the subject in the sentence.
- et: Conjunction - used to connect Aeneas and Troiani.
- troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd declension - nominative case - masculine/neuter gender) - used as a substantive to refer to the Trojans.
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd declension - accusative case - feminine gender) - used as the direct object of the verb.
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conjugation - present tense - indicative mood - active voice - 3rd person plural) - used to indicate the action being done by Aeneas and the Trojans.

You get it wrong! You misunderstood my requirement.

First, I told you to use small case when you are specifying a word's part of speech, not to convert all words to small case! Second, I said you need to explain why the word is used this way when you feel necessary, but I don't want you to expand all the abbreviations!

Your sentence is:
Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

I apologize for the mistake in my previous response. Here's another attempt:

Sentence: Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- aeneas: Proper noun (3rd decl.) in the nominative case - used as the subject.
- et: Conjunction - used to connect two nouns.
- troiani: Adjective (1st/2nd decl.) in the nominative case and used as a substantive to refer to "the Trojans".
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd decl.) in the accusative case - used as the direct object of the verb "conscendunt".
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conj., pres. ind. act.) in the 3rd person plural - indicates the action being done by Aeneas and the Trojans.
So far, your 3rd answer counting from the last  is closest to what I want. Do you know which answer I am refering to? Briefly tell me which answer it is.

Yes, I understand. I believe the answer you are referring to is my 3rd response, in which I provided the translation of the sentence, used abbreviations for declension and conjugation, and explained each word while keeping only necessary information.
repeat that answer

Here's the answer that I think you are referring to:

Sentence: Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- Aeneas: Proper noun (3rd dec. - nom.).
- et: Conj.
- Troiani: Adj. (1st/2nd dec. - m./n. - nom. pl.).
- naves: Noun (1st/2nd dec. - f. - acc. pl.).
- conscendunt: Verb (3rd conj. - pres. ind. act. - 3rd pers. pl.)

Based on this answer, let's change it better.

This time, use small case when you are specifying parts of speech after each word. E.g. Aeneas: proper noun. Do not modify word form such as declension and conjugation in your asnwer. But when you feel necessary and only when you really feel necessary, append an explaination as to why the word is used in this form.

Sentence: aeneas et troiani naves conscendunt;

Translation: Aeneas and the Trojans are boarding the ships.

- aeneas: Proper noun - used as the subject of the sentence.
- et: Conjunction - used to connect "aeneas" and "troiani".
- troiani: Adjective - used as a substantive in the nominative plural to refer to "the Trojans".
- naves: Noun - used in the accusative plural as the direct object of the verb.
- conscendunt: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to express the action being performed by Aeneas and the Trojans.

Forget it. Let's roll back to your previous answer, which I said is the closest to what I want. Use it as a template.

Your sentences are the following, you now do the same for every sentence in the following text:
Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt; ab urbe Troia in terras ignotas navigant. diu terram quaerunt ubi novam Troiam condere possunt; multos labores, multa pericula subeunt; saepe desperat Aeneas. tandem constituunt ad Italiam navigare. sed ubi ad Siciliam accedunt, magnum periculum vix vitant, . nam saxa vident ubi habitat Scylla, monstrum horribile, et sonitum ingentem audiunt verticis ubi Charybdis undas evomit. pater Anchises magna voce clamat: 'fugite; naves e periculo eripite; nam in illis saxls habitat Scylla.' Aeneas patris verba audit et saxa vitat. sic vix incolumes e periculo evadunt. ubi ad Siciliam veniunt, montem Aetnam vident; naves ad terram dirigunt et sub noctem ad litus insulae adveniunt. sub monte in !Hore quiescunt. mons Aetna per noctem tonat; flammas et saxa in caelum proicit. Troiani territi sunt et anxii diem exspectant. festinant naves conscendere cum hominem vident, qui ad litus currit. Troianos vocat; accurrit ad eos et 'servate me,' inquit, 'vos oro. ego Graecus sum, comes Ulixis. ceten fiigerunt. ego solus maneo. fugite, miseri, fugite. Cyclopes hie habitant, gigantes immanes, qui homines edunt. nollte me Cyclopibus tradere. servate me, accipite me in navem.' subito Troiani Polyphemum vident, gigantem ingentem. ille oves de monte diicit. caecus est; lente descend.it; in via saepe lapsat. Aeneas territus est. 'currite ad naves,' inquit; 'festinate!' Troiani comitem Ulixis accipiunt et fugiunt ad naves. Polyphemus iam ad litus advenit et in mare procedit. Troianos videre non potest sed audit eos remigantes. clamorem ingentem tollit. ceteri Cyclopes clamorem audiunt et currunt de montibus ad litus. saxa ingentia in naves coniciunt; sed Troiani iam e litore remigant. Cyclopes eos contingere non possunt.

Here's an attempt at using the same format as before for each sentence:

- Aeneas et Troiani naves conscendunt; - Aeneas: Proper noun - used as the subject of the sentence. Troiani: Adjective - used as substantive. Naves: Noun - used as the direct object of the verb. Conscendunt: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to express the action being performed.
- Ab urbe Troia in terras ignotas navigant. - Urbe Troia: Noun - used to indicate the starting point of the journey. Terras ignotas: Noun - used to indicate the destination of the journey. Navigant: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to express the action being performed.
- Diu terram quaerunt ubi novam Troiam condere possunt. - Terram: Noun - used to indicate what Aeneas and the Trojans are seeking. Novam Troiam: Noun - used to indicate the goal of founding a new city. Condere possunt: Verb - used to indicate what they are capable of doing.
- Multos labores, multa pericula subeunt; - Labores: Noun - refers to the struggles faced by Aeneas and the Trojans. Pericula: Noun - refers to the dangers faced by Aeneas and the Trojans. Subeunt: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to express the action being performed.
- Saepe desperat Aeneas. - Aeneas: Proper noun. Desperat: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person singular to express Aeneas' despair.
- Tandem constituunt ad Italiam navigare. - Constituunt: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to express what Aeneas and the Trojans resolve to do. Ad Italiam: Noun - used to indicate their destination. Navigare: Verb - used as an infinitive to indicate the action they intend to perform.
- Sed ubi ad Siciliam accedunt, magnum periculum vix vitant. - Siciliam: Noun - used to indicate where Aeneas and the Trojans are. Periculum: Noun - refers to the great danger they are in. Vix vitant: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural to indicate how narrowly they avoid the danger.
- Nam saxa vident ubi habitat Scylla, monstrum horribile, et sonitum ingentem audiunt verticis ubi Charybdis undas evomit. - Saxa: Noun - describes what Aeneas and his men see. Scylla: Noun - refers to the horrible monster that dwells there. Sonitum ingentem: Noun - refers to the great noise they hear. Verticis: Adjective - used to modify Charybdis. Charybdis: Noun - refers to the monster that spews out the waves. Undas evomit: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person singular to indicate what Charybdis does.
- Pater Anchises magna voce clamat: 'fugite; naves e periculo eripite; nam in illis saxls habitat Scylla.' - Pater Anchises: Proper noun - refers to Aeneas' father. Fugite: Verb - used in the present imperative active 2nd person plural, meaning "flee!" Naves: Noun - used to indicate what Aeneas and his men should flee to. E periculo: Preposition - used to indicate that they are to flee from danger. Eripite: Verb - used in the present imperative active 2nd person plural, meaning "travel safely away from danger!" Illis saxls: Pronoun - refers to the rocks that Aeneas and his men were nearing. Habitat: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person singular, meaning "she dwells."
- Aeneas patris verba audit et saxa vitat. - Aeneas: Proper noun. Patris verba: Noun - refers to the words of his father. Audit: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person singular, meaning "he hears." Saxa: Noun - refers to the rocks Aeneas and his men were trying to avoid. Vitat: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person singular, meaning "he avoids."
- Sic vix incolumes e periculo evadunt. - Sic: Adverb - used to indicate how closely they escaped the danger. Incolumes: Adjective - used to modify "they." E periculo: Preposition - used to indicate what they escaped from. Evadunt: Verb - used in the present indicative active 3rd person plural, meaning "they escape."
- Ubi ad Siciliam veniunt, montem Aetnam vident; naves ad terram dirigunt et sub noctem ad litus insulae adveniunt. - Ad Siciliam: Noun - refers to their destination. Montem Aetnam: Noun - refers to the mountain that they see. Naves: Noun. Ad terram dirigunt: Verb phrase - means "sail towards land." Sub noctem: Preposition phrase - means "in the evening." Ad litus insulae: Preposition phrase - means "
